ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for orange-flavoured carbonated soda; the closest defensible parent class 'carbonated soft drink' (FOODON_03301710) is used as a closest_parent match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A003K covers carbonated soft drinks broadly; no orange-soda-specific code exists in the public FoodEx2 hierarchy. Used as a closest_parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged orange carbonated soda; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve 330 ml can of packaged orange soda, equivalent to approximately 330 g, is the most common retail portion consumed in one sitting.

nutrient_source.title: Orange carbonated soft drink — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient composition database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id D06020024 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this source without modification.

gi_source.title: Fanta Orange (carbonated orange soft drink) — GI value 68 ± 8; analogous to generic packaged orange carbonated soda
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for a generic 'orange soda (packaged)'. The closest published analogous entry is Fanta Orange (a commercially produced carbonated orange-flavoured soft drink), which has a reported GI of 68 (medium). This is used as the analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 330 g serving = (68 × 8.59 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.30) / 100 = approximately 19.8. The input record's embedded GI value of 43 (low) with confidence 0.409 was reviewed and rejected as it lacks an approved source citation; the University of Sydney analogous estimate of 68 is adopted instead.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.89 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage; liquid sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently elevates blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid packaged orange soda entirely; the 9.89 g/100 g sugar load in a liquid matrix is an independent blood-pressure risk factor per AHA 2018 guidelines, and a 330 ml can delivers approximately 33 g of added sugar with no compensating nutrients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.89 g sugar/100 g, GI 68, zero dietary fibre — liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose excursion with no modulating factors
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid packaged orange soda; it is a classic sugar-sweetened beverage with a medium-to-high glycaemic index (GI 68), zero fibre, and a 330 ml serving delivering approximately 33 g of rapidly absorbed sugar that produces an acute postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed sugar-sweetened beverage; regular consumption is associated with increased triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular event risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should eliminate packaged orange soda from their diet; habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is independently associated with elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and increased cardiovascular mortality risk per prospective cohort evidence (Malik et al., Circulation 2010).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (9.89 g/100 g), zero fibre, no anti-inflammatory nutrients — promotes AGE formation and pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid packaged orange soda; its high sugar content promotes advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation and inflammatory cytokine activity, with no anti-inflammatory nutrients to offset the pro-inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Orange soda (packaged) is a commercially produced, ultra-processed carbonated soft drink flavoured with orange essence or artificial orange flavouring. It is sold in cans (typically 330 ml) and bottles across global markets including Singapore. The beverage is characterised by its bright orange colour, sweet citrus taste, and effervescent texture from dissolved carbon dioxide. It contains no real fruit juice in standard formulations and derives its sweetness entirely from added sugar (approximately 9.89 g per 100 ml). Per 100 ml, it provides 36 kcal, 0.07 g protein, 0.01 g fat, and 8.59 g carbohydrate, with 8 mg sodium and negligible dietary fibre. From a clinical nutrition perspective, orange soda is a classic sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is contraindicated for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. A single 330 ml can delivers approximately 33 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with a glycaemic index of approximately 68 (medium), producing a significant postprandial glucose excursion. The absence of fibre, protein, vitamins, and minerals means it provides no nutritional benefit. Public health guidelines in Singapore (HPB) and internationally (WHO, AHA) recommend limiting or eliminating SSB consumption. Healthier alternatives include plain sparkling water, unsweetened oolong tea, or infused water with fresh citrus.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id D06020024, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). GI value estimated from the closest University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry (Fanta Orange, GI 68) with documented substitution rationale. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a commercially produced carbonated orange soft drink.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — orange soda contains no fat-derived saturated fatty acids by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source — carbonated soft drinks contain no dietary fibre. calcium_mg is 0.0 and iron_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this packaged orange soda are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D06020024. The input record's embedded GI value of 43 (low, confidence 0.409, source 'Embedded GI Database') was rejected as it does not cite an approved source; the University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate of GI 68 (Fanta Orange) is adopted. GL per serving calculated as (68 × 8.59 / 100) × 3.30 = 19.8. The dietary_tag 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section is borderline — at 36 kcal/100 ml it is low in absolute terms but the sugar density makes it nutritionally poor; this tag is not carried forward to clinical guidance. protein_g (0.07) and fat_g (0.01) are non-zero trace values from HPB source and do not require zero-macro justification.
